speaker: interviewer
text: An interview with Robert Ness on October the 31st, 1995, focusing on songs that Mr. Ness remembers from the ghettos and the camps. We were speaking about [NON-ENGLISH]. Would you mind singing that song?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] I gave you the content before. But there is this passage, a fragment of it about where is-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your wife? Where is your child? Where is your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH] And it goes. It repeats again about they would have gone to the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the death march, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes the death march. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember singing this song in the Vilna ghetto to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know I learn it in the Vilna ghetto. There's no question about it. I might have been singing it in Estonia, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were other songs that you learned from the theater in the ghetto, that you remember fragments of.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: One you mentioned was a song about bombs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Well, I don't know whether that necessarily bombs was in the theater. But it's a possibility. But the fragments that I know about it, it's inmates are hoping for the bombs to come down. Because at one time, I remember there was an alarm. There were some Russian planes, Soviet planes. They came in the city of Vilnius, throw a few bombs there. And I wouldn't be surprised if the song was kind of created at that time. And it goes about-- bombs, come down. Come down. The guards, the Lithuanian guards at the gates are strong as walls. And bombs, come. Start falling. That's all I remember from it. And it goes-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [YIDDISH], it means the plural of bomb in Yiddish. And so it's a song of revenge, of a hope for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Yes. In other words, it's OK. If we go on, then we go on there too. But come on. Let's give it to them. And like all the other songs from the Vilna ghetto that you've sung, this is Yiddish. This is a Yiddish song. Yes, that's a Yiddish song. Yes. Yes. There's a general partisan song. But these are well known. But there are--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was another song, children's years, a fragment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fragments, yes. I know the melody, which starts, [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did I lose you? Where did I lose you? And I know that [NON-ENGLISH] has it in this book. It's [NON-ENGLISH]. And so I presume that is a song. I don't remember more than that, just the melody starting. [NON-ENGLISH], children's year, where did I lose you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't remember further than that. And it made an impression on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Sure. Because I was myself 14 years old at the time. Most of the songs, as you know most probably, it's the Vilna ghetto. Vilna before the war was considered the Jerusalem of Lithuania. In other words, there were publishing houses. There was a rich culture. It was a little country by itself, kind of a Jewish communities that had its own administrations, its own songs, theaters, publication. I mean, it was vivid and it was live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's obviously, they had the people to produce stuff, and to make songs, and write songs. The writers were there. The poets were there. So it wasn't the same, like some small town like Slonim or even Grodno. It was a bigger town. But it didn't have the material. It didn't have the substance that Vilna had. There was another fragment of a song which you remember, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't remember that part. But I know that [NON-ENGLISH] had it. I gave her the page, [NON-ENGLISH]. And but it's all I remember. It's just it's quieter, quieter. It's like it's talking about the Ponar, where the Jews were killed. Where the grass is growing, be quiet. It's just the winds are bowing through. It's kind of like holy ground. It's where they murdered the people who were killed out there. I don't remember more than that. That's one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was another song, 'Not Your Luck.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [NON-ENGLISH] Now, this is a story about people used to go out to work. And everybody used to go out. You used to have a brigadier. In other words, the eldest, the Jewish eldest, who was responsible for the work of getting him out--

speaker: interviewer
text: A kapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kind of like a kapo in the concentration camp. But in Vilna in the ghetto they didn't it call it a kapo. They called it a brigadier. So this one, it's just fragments. And it goes-- one part starts in Russian where they ask from the brigadier. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me a furlough for about 25 days. Now this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Russian, that particular part. But then it goes in Yiddish. It says, [SPEAKING YIDDISH] It's not your luck to be free. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] There is no furlough. Don't talk yourself into some stories that aren't there. So this part, it's all I remember. And it goes-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH] We need a full number of people in the working force. And then in connection with that one is this little girl who is kind of nosy. And she stays at the gate. And she wants to know who is there, and who's coming, and what it is. And all she hears is no. Don't stay here. And don't poke in there. And don't stick your nose into that. And it's kind of cute. And that kind of caught it to you. It goes Susie. Her name is Susie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is another song--

speaker: interviewee
text: Another, or it could be part of the brigadier song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Part of the 'Not Your Luck.'

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Not Your Luck.' But I think it's a separate one which is called 'Susie.' The girl's name is Susie. And she says, [SINGING IN YIDDISH] They call me Susie. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] Like I told you, it said, don't stay here. Don't go there. Don't stick your nose into something. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] Somebody's standing at the gate. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] I want to know who it is. That's as far as I remember. But it was a cute song. It really was, kind of a humorous, showing a child with innocent eyes looking at things. And it just gets no, no, no, no, no. And he wants to know what it's all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a song in the theater also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, definitely. 'Susie' in the theater, no question about it, no question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Moshe, Hold On. Don't Give Up.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now that is a cute one too. It's also I know only parts of it. But that is definitely in the theater because I remember the broom. And it says, it says basically hold on. Don't fall apart. Hey, we have to get out. And you can never tell where is the beginning, where is the middle, where is the end. If God wants to, a broom can shoot. Here, at this moment, it sweeps. And suddenly it starts. Now, that's all that I remember from it. But it was a nice song, a lively one. And it goes, [SINGING IN YIDDISH] OK. So that was part of the song. It was a live one, kind of giving a little bit of encouragement that it's not the end. You can never tell. Things are changing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to keep hoping.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. Keep up. Keep moving. That's right. Stay awake. Stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And 'Quiet, The Night Is Full of Stars.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a partisan song. But it was already-- say, I learned it in the ghetto Vilna. It's a Yiddish partisan song. And it's about a fellow who teaches the girl how to use a pistol. And she goes out on a reconnaissance and gets the first time in battle. And she says, for our generation and for generations to come. So that is really a live one. But I don't remember most of the part. I do remember this fragment about-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know that line. Then it goes--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how I taught you? Do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH] You see, I don't remember too much. But that is-- I do remember that song. And [NON-ENGLISH] has it about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a chance that that was actually sung in the theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: I doubt it. I don't remember. But when I saw [NON-ENGLISH] being on the stage and fighting a Roman, which was as obvious as can be with the allegory on the Nazi Germany and Germans, I doubt it. I doubt it. It's probably not. But I know it was a partisan song. I doubt that it was played in the theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with partisans while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but except my sister and brother, future brother-in-law, got out of the ghetto. And somebody came from the partisan and picked them up. But I'll tell you a nice story about this fellow. This is really hilarious now that you mention it. It has nothing to do with singing. I visited Israel in 1957, around-- yes, 57 must've been. And we're on the road from the Galilee, a very narrow road and the mountains of the Galilee on the way to Nahariya. And we are in a cab of a friend of ours who was in the ghetto Vilna with us who was in the partisans, who went out from Grodno, Vilna, and got into the partisans, who was a soldier in the Polish army when the war started. And he lost an eye in the 1939 war. Hershel Lipshits. He's dead now. And so we are driving and he had a cab. And he took us for a ride. And suddenly there was a bus behind us. And he blows the horn, and blows the horn. Well, there is not room. And the road is so narrow in the mountains. You fool around too much and you'll be overboard. But finally we get on the closer, already where the road is straight. And the bus passes us up. And he goes in front of us. And Hershel says, watch. He's going to stop. He wants us to hit him in the back to pay me back for holding him back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sure enough, the guy puts on the brakes on the bus. But Hershel, sharp he was-- and he stops. And driver goes out from the bus, the bus driver. And he starts off. What is it with you? Because he see his Tel Aviv license plates. Mind you, we are on the north of the country. And called him, you Tel Aviv donkey, your mother Tel Aviv donkey. [NON-ENGLISH]. What is it? The whole road is for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You cannot pass, and so on. And Hershel looks at him. And he starts screaming at him. And Hershel says, [NON-ENGLISH] Motke. Motke, I know you. [NON-ENGLISH]. Motke. And he continues. He says, Motke, you took us out from the ghetto. Well, these guys start-- Motke calls him redhead. He's a redhead. And before it's over, they start kissing and hugging one another. He was the one who took out him, my sister, my brother-in-law from the ghetto of Vilna to the partisan. That is really only in Israel things like this can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this, I never forgot that. Motke [NON-ENGLISH]. Motke. Motke. He did-- that guy screams. And you, you idiot. What do you mean you tongue donkey. Don't you see that there is more room?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, [? Motke ?] [NON-ENGLISH]. Motke, [NON-ENGLISH]. I know you. I know you. I remember you. And before you know, it's this whole party. Oh, the people in the bus clapping and laughing and so forth. It was quite an experience. So this this was a fellow that took out my-- from the ghetto. So there was some connection. People were going out.

speaker: interviewer
text: One never knows who you're going to meet. Yes. But oh, that was really something. That Motke. So I met him. And then of course, and there was kissing and hugging and so forth. From Vilna, you were sent to Estonia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I run away at once with my father. But there was some people-- I mentioned it to you in my other testimony, I think, when I was hiding there. And then they were caught. And I was going back to Vilna. And I came back to the ghetto. And I was caught on September the 3rd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken to Estonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sent to-- The first place was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first place, yes. That's right. Came to Ereda in Estonia, a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there is a song--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Ereda, the slave labor camp in Estonia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And you see, you have to imagine the camp. Later on when I came back to Ereda in 1944, the camp was already on a hill. And the lower part was kind of dumped. And people weren't there anymore. But it was slum--

speaker: interviewer
text: A swamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Swamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I first came, absolutely. There was a little bit of a thing that was. But you used to put in a foot. And you used to go in with it. And it was really bad. I had boots, believe it or not, that I had came with it. And it was still holding it. So then this respect it was OK. But it was just terrible there. Just on swamps, plain, simple swamps. It's just, you work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this particular song, it's in the nature of [RUSSIAN]. That's the Russian word, limericks. And it's the melody from the limericks, the Russian image. And it goes lalala lalala la. Lalalala lalalala. And this is in Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm just telling you the melody is from the Russian [RUSSIAN], the limericks. And it was telling stories about-- I don't remember all of it. It's fragments, of course. But it tells a story in the camp that the little fish swim around, making the fish to see if they are inmates of the camp. But there are big fish and little fish. And the big fish eat the little fish. But shiny, better days are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a hopeful--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, very much so, very much so, very much so, indeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it go?

speaker: interviewee
text: It goes like this. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, what kind of a fish is it that it-- the big shot that it swims in the camp? It says, oh, it's a fish that swims around in the camp here and there, but doesn't get slim from it. Do you remember singing this in the camp itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. It was longer than that. It was a story because families were broken up. And this was a camp together, women and men. The women had separate barracks. So the cook, he was married. But he was living with some wife from some other man in Vilna. So there were stories about him and her. I don't remember the names. But it was kind of a local type of a story from the camp. And it was in Ereda. And it starts, in Ereda [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a song that did give some encouragement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was a lively song, kind of forgetting the trouble, making jokes of the administration of the camp, or of the big shots.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sung at work and in the barracks as well, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably. I knew this song. I think other people knew it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in other slave labor camps in Estonia. And there was a song that you remember called [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, you see, I was never in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a mining camp. And what they used to do is mine the shales, for oil shales, not for coal. Now, a friend of mine who is now a professor of mathematics at the University of-- Delaware University. And I talked to him. He was there. And he was in the mines with his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked him. I said, didn't you ever hear that song? He goes, no. No. I never heard it from anybody. And it just so happens that my sister liked that song. I was driving once on business some years ago from Phoenix to Las Vegas. So I was singing all kind of songs. I was singing Russian songs, German songs, Polish songs, Yiddish songs. So I came up with one. Oh, she liked it so much. She was driving me lulu on it. So finally-- she was married to [INAUDIBLE]. So she asked me would I sing. So I wrote it up in song form with all the punctuation with the whole spiel, and put it on the tape, and send it to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they liked it so much. And then they give it somebody else. And then they put it in [INAUDIBLE]. And it's in there. But it got buried. And that was it. But basically, what I remember goes like this. OK. The song itself, it talks about the yearning for the Vilna that he knew, his homeland, how nice it is. Here, he's working in mines. And he's working hard. And comes the night, his eyes are closing on him. And when he has a free moment, he thinks of Vilna. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] It's a nice song. The melody is nice too. And it's lifting up too. He's dreaming of the Vilna that he knows, that it's now covered in snow and so forth. But it is from [NON-ENGLISH]. And I am talking about a friend who was there. He doesn't remember. He has never heard it. I heard it. I sang it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You might have sung it and you might have learned it in Kuremae.

speaker: interviewee
text: Possible, because the transfer from one camp to another. Other people come from other camps. It's no question about it that as a matter of fact, you remember I told you once that I was kind of a hootenanny, so to speak, what you would call it here, where they gather on a Sunday. And now I'm talking summer 1944--

speaker: interviewer
text: In which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ereda.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ereda?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ereda, the camp on top. And then there was a singing. [IMITATES SINGING] OK. So I come. And I come out there too. And I take it a high key. And I couldn't hold it. And then I quit in the middle and started all over again. And everybody bursted out laughing. OK. So I'm sure that this song might have been among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among the songs that you sang in Ereda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes, yes yes. I am sure. Yes. OK. I sang a number of songs. I don't remember exactly. But it wouldn't surprise me a bit that that was one of them that I sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the other songs at all in the hootenanny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't called-- I just gave it the description as hootenanny. I must have sang the [NON-ENGLISH]. That went through well. I know that. Maybe that was the one that I took it too high and I couldn't hold it. And I started from scratch. I don't remember. I sang a number of songs. But there were other people who were singing too. It was kind of a Sunday. People didn't go to work. And it was summer. And it was already-- the camp was going to be liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's going to be moved. And there were rumors that we're going to be taken to Germany or to Tallinn. Some people believed it. Some didn't. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I was supposed to run away from that camp. But the guy finally walked out. And I was stuck there. But anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: But the songs, the songs, you do remember singing the songs. And it was uplifting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's no question about it, no question about it. That particular, quote-unquote, 'hootenanny' was, I would say, at least 300, 400 people minimum, if not more. They made a stage, a platform. There were no speakers or loud-- you didn't have any magnifying singing. It's just your voice. You had to carry it. My voice was pretty good. So it carried it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a nice diversion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it certainly was. That was in the summer of 1944. I remember it distinctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ereda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Germans listening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the guards around it. But the guards weren't German. The guards were-- at that time I think the guards were Estonian. Yeah. I think, pretty sure they were Estonian guards.
